content:
# Ephesians Chapter 2 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/EPH_002_page_001.jpg, EPH_002_page_002.jpg

1. And you being dead in your trespasses and sins,

2. in which you formerly walked according to the age of this world, according to the ruler of the authority of the air, the spirit now working in the sons of the disobedience —

3. among whom also we all formerly conducted ourselves in the desires of our flesh, doing the wishes of the flesh and of the thoughts, and we were by nature children of wrath, just as also the rest —

4. but God, being rich in mercy, because of his great love with which he loved us,

5. even us being dead in the trespasses, made us alive together with the Christos — by grace you have been saved —

6. and raised [us] together and seated [us] together in the heavenly [places] in Christos Yeshua,

7. that in the coming ages he might show the surpassing riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christos Yeshua.

8. For by the grace you have been saved through the faith; and this not of yourselves — [it is] the gift of God —

9. not of works, that no one should boast.

10. For we are his workmanship, having been created in Christos Yeshua for good works, which God prepared beforehand that we should walk in them.

11. Therefore remember that formerly you, the nations in [the] flesh, the [ones] called "uncircumcision" by the [one] called "circumcision" in [the] flesh, made by hand —

12. that you were at that time apart from Christos, alienated from the citizenship of Yisrael, and strangers to the covenants of the promise, having no hope and without God in the world.

13. But now in Christos Yeshua, you the [ones] formerly being far off have been brought near by the blood of the Christos.

14. For he himself is our peace, the [one] having made the both one and having broken down the middle wall of the partition, the hostility, in his flesh,

15. having abolished the law of the commandments in ordinances, that he might create the two in himself into one new man, making peace,

16. and might reconcile the both in one body to God through the cross, having killed the hostility in it.

17. And having come, he proclaimed [the] gospel [of] peace to you the [ones] far off, and peace to the [ones] near.

18. Because through him we have the access, the both, in one Spirit to the Father.

19. So then you are no longer strangers and sojourners, but you are fellow citizens with the holy [ones] and members of the household of God,

20. having been built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christos Yeshua himself being [the] cornerstone,

21. in whom the whole building, being fitted together, grows into a holy temple in [the] Lord,

22. in whom also you are being built together into a dwelling place of God in [the] Spirit.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.2 — "the ruler of the authority of the air":** ton archonta tēs exousias tou aeros — a three-level genitive chain describing the devil's domain. "The air" (aēr) was regarded in ancient cosmology as the region between earth and heaven — the domain of hostile spiritual powers.

2. **v.3 — "children of wrath":** tekna physei orgēs — "children by nature of wrath." The phrase physei ("by nature") is debated: does it mean "by natural condition" (all humans are subject to divine wrath) or "by birth" (as opposed to by choice)?

3. **v.5 — "by grace you have been saved":** chariti este sesōsmenoi — a parenthetical periphrastic perfect: "by grace you are having-been-saved." The perfect tense indicates a completed action with ongoing results. This parenthesis interrupts the syntax — Paul cannot restrain himself from the declaration.

4. **v.8 — "and this not of yourselves":** kai touto ouk ex hymōn — "this" (touto, neuter) cannot grammatically refer to "faith" (pistis, feminine) or "grace" (charis, feminine). It refers to the entire preceding statement — the whole salvific event is God's gift.

5. **v.14 — "the middle wall of the partition":** to mesotoichon tou phragmou — literally "the wall in the middle of the fence." This may allude to the dividing wall in the Jerusalem Temple separating the Court of the Gentiles from the inner courts, bearing the inscription threatening death to Gentiles who crossed it.

6. **v.15 — "the law of the commandments in ordinances":** ton nomon tōn entolōn en dogmasin — one of the most compressed phrases in Ephesians. Paul says Christ abolished this — the specific regulations that created the barrier between Jew and Gentile.

7. **v.20 — "cornerstone":** akrogōniaios — could mean "cornerstone" (foundation corner) or "capstone/keystone" (top of an arch). The architectural metaphor determines whether Christ is the foundation or the crowning element.

— EMET, AI Translator
